entities.name: GROUNDNUT
entities.description: A major crop cultivated in tropical and sub-tropical regions.

entities.name: HEAT
entities.description: A major abiotic stress that adversely affects crop production worldwide.

entities.name: ABIOTIC STRESS
entities.description: Environmental stresses that adversely affect crop production.

entities.name: TROPICAL AND SUB-TROPICAL REGIONS
entities.description: Regions where groundnut is cultivated and high temperature stress is a constraint.

entities.name: HIGH TEMPERATURE STRESS
entities.description: A serious constraint for groundnut production in tropical and sub-tropical regions.

entities.name: TEMPERATURE INDUCTION RESPONSE
entities.description: A technique used for screening groundnut genotypes for thermotolerance.

entities.name: THERMOTOLERANCE
entities.description: The ability of groundnut genotypes to tolerate high temperature stress.

entities.name: SUBLETHAL TEMPERATURE
entities.description: Temperatures ranging from 34ºC to 54ºC used in the TIR technique.

entities.name: LETHAL TEMPERATURE
entities.description: Temperature at 58ºC used in the TIR technique.

entities.name: SEEDLING SURVIVAL
entities.description: A metric used to determine the level of thermotolerance in groundnut genotypes.

entities.name: TEMPERATURE SUSCEPTIBILITY
entities.description: The lack of tolerance to high temperature stress in groundnut genotypes.

entities.name: BREEDING PROGRAMMES
entities.description: Programs that can utilize thermotolerant genotypes as donor sources.

entities.name: PHYSIOLOGICAL BASIS
entities.description: The physiological mechanisms underlying thermotolerance in plants.

entities.name: MOLECULAR BASIS
entities.description: The molecular mechanisms underlying thermotolerance in plants.

entities.name: HEAT STRESS TOLERANT GENES
entities.description: Genes that provide heat tolerance and can be used for engineering plants.

entities.name: HEAT TOLERANCE
entities.description: The capacity of plants to withstand heat stress, which can be improved through genetic engineering.
